topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email hit my screen like a physical blow. A sterile rejection, a polite dismissal of months of grueling effort. I stared at the words—"We regret to inform you that your application..."—and a hot, prickly sensation began to bloom in my chest. My hands, gripping the mouse, started to tremble violently. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet dorm room. I stood up, pacing the small space like a caged animal. Every surface seemed to vibrate with the sheer injustice of it all. I kicked at the worn carpet, the frustration coiling tight in my throat until I felt a pressure building behind my eyes. I needed to scream, to shatter something, anything, to release this suffocating feeling of being utterly dismissed.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection letter for the tenth time, my jaw clenched so tight my molars ached. The words swam before my eyes, mocking the dedication I had poured into those essays, the late nights fueled by caffeine and sheer stubbornness. A low growl started deep in my abdomen. I shoved the papers across the desk, scattering textbooks and notes into a chaotic mess. My breath came in ragged, shallow gasps. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus grounds, feeling a surge of intense, burning heat rise up my neck. I wanted to punch the glass, to make a noise loud enough to shake the foundations of the building. My knuckles were white where I gripped the windowsill, a tremor running through my entire body that refused to subside.

[story 3]

"No," I whispered, the sound raspy and unfamiliar. The word felt thin, insufficient against the weight of the denial. I paced back and forth across the common room floor, my movements sharp and jerky. My vision tunneled slightly, everything outside the circle of my immediate distress blurring into a dull, hostile haze. I stopped abruptly, spinning around to face the empty room, my chest heaving with rapid, shallow breaths. A sharp, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a harsh, choked noise that was more animal than human. I clenched my fists until the skin around my knuckles turned painfully white. I felt a desperate, consuming need to overturn the desk, to make a spectacle of this profound disrespect, anything to puncture this suffocating silence of failure.
